topic: Two strangers realize they've been dating the same person
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with a low, irritating buzz. Sarah tapped her foot against the tiled floor, the rhythm a sharp, restless counterpoint to the gentle clatter of mugs. She checked her watch for the third time in as many minutes. When the barista called out her name, she didn't even bother to look up immediately, just snatched the cup, her jaw tight. Across the room, leaning against the pastry display, was a man with a familiar, infuriatingly charming smile. He was laughing at something a woman beside him had said, and the way he tilted his head, the slight crinkle near his eyes—it hit Sarah like a physical jolt. Her hand froze halfway to the sugar packet. *No way. That can’t be.* She forced herself to look, and the world seemed to tilt. It was him. The man from her last disastrous date six months ago. The one whose profile picture she’d scrolled past a thousand times. A sudden, hot wave of frustration washed over her; why was this happening *now*?

[story 2]

Mark paced the length of the sterile waiting room. Every minute dragged, stretching the silence between the automated announcements into an unbearable vacuum. He shifted his weight from one foot to the other, his knuckles white where he gripped the strap of his worn messenger bag. He was supposed to be meeting a colleague, but his mind kept snagging on a face he couldn't quite place, yet felt intensely known. Then, across the room, a woman walked in, and the air seemed to compress. Her posture, the way she tucked a strand of dark hair behind her ear—it was an exact mirror of someone he’d spent the last few weeks agonizing over. A muscle ticked violently in his jaw. He stopped pacing, his breath catching in his throat. He watched her move toward the seating area, and the realization hit him with the force of a physical blow: that smile, that specific tilt of the head. It was the same one he’d seen in a blurry photograph, the one that had haunted his late-night scrolling. He felt a sudden, sharp need to bolt, to escape the suffocating recognition.

[story 3]

Liam stared at the flickering neon sign outside the late-night diner. He drummed his fingers rapidly on the laminated tabletop, the sound a frantic percussion against the quiet hum of the ventilation system. He was waiting for someone, anyone, but the anticipation was curdling into sheer annoyance. He kept glancing toward the entrance, a tight knot forming in his stomach. Then, she appeared. She slid into the booth opposite him, and the immediate familiarity was startling. The way she leaned forward, her expression shifting into that familiar, easygoing smirk—it was undeniable. Liam’s entire body seemed to tense up. He couldn't look away, his gaze locked onto her profile. The details were too precise, too layered. A deep, simmering irritation rose within him; this was too much coincidence, too much uncanny alignment. He wanted to dissect the situation, to demand an explanation, but he could only sit there, his gaze burning into hers, completely unable to move.
